subtopic: Small Finance Banks and PSL Norms
title: Small Finance Banks (SFBs) and Universal Bank Transition
focus: Eligibility criteria for SFBs to transition to Universal Banks and foundational facts about SFBs.
rationale: Covers the specific RBI criteria for SFB transition and the basic regulatory framework of SFBs, which are highly testable for UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI granted 'in-principle' approval to AU Small Finance Bank to transition to a universal bank. Eligibility criteria include scheduled status for 5 years, stock listing, minimum net worth of ₹1,000 crore, net profits in the last two FYs, and gross/net NPAs ≤3% and ≤1% respectively. SFBs were announced in the 2014-15 budget, registered under the Companies Act 2013, and licensed under Section 22 of the Banking Regulation Act 1949. Minimum paid-up equity capital is ₹200 crore (₹100 crore initially for UCBs transitioning to SFBs).
evidenceBullets: Transition requires 5 years of scheduled status, ₹1,000 crore minimum net worth, and stock exchange listing.
Asset quality for transition requires Gross NPA ≤ 3% and Net NPA ≤ 1% over the last two FYs.
SFBs are registered under the Companies Act, 2013 and licensed under Section 22 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
Minimum paid-up voting equity capital for SFBs is ₹200 crores.

subtopic: Small Finance Banks and PSL Norms
title: Priority Sector Lending (PSL) Norms for SFBs
focus: Changes in PSL targets for SFBs and the general framework of Priority Sector Lending.
rationale: PSL targets are frequently asked in UPSC. The reduction in the PSL target for SFBs from 75% to 60% is a significant policy shift.
sectionExcerpt: RBI issued new PSL rules for SFBs under Section 22(1) of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. Effective FY 2025-26, the total PSL requirement for SFBs is reduced from 75% to 60% of Adjusted Net Bank Credit (ANBC). The 40% mandatory allocation remains, but the flexible allocation is reduced to 20% (usable for non-PSL secured loans). PSL was established in the 1970s and applies to Commercial Banks, RRBs, SFBs, LABs, and UCBs. Categories include Agriculture, MSME, Export Credit, Education, Housing, Social Infrastructure, and Renewable Energy.
evidenceBullets: New PSL target for SFBs is reduced to 60% of ANBC (effective FY 2025-26), down from the earlier 75%.
The 60% target comprises 40% mandatory allocation to PSL sectors and 20% flexible allocation.
PSL framework applies to Commercial Banks, RRBs, SFBs, Local Area Banks, and Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks.
PSL categories include Agriculture, MSME, Export Credit, Education, Housing, Social Infrastructure, and Renewable Energy.

subtopic: Small Finance Banks and PSL Norms
title: SARFAESI Act, 2002 and NPA Recovery
focus: Anomalies in the SARFAESI Act regarding borrower redemption rights and the mechanisms for NPA recovery.
rationale: The Supreme Court's observation on the contradiction between Section 13(8) and Rules 8 & 9 makes this a prime candidate for legal/economic questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Supreme Court highlighted a contradiction in the SARFAESI Act, 2002: Section 13(8) states borrowers lose the right to redeem a secured asset once an auction notice is published, whereas Rules 8 & 9 allow redemption up until the auction date. The Act aims to recover NPAs (loans overdue for >90 days) for financial institutions. Key features include a mandatory 60-day demand notice to borrowers and recovery methods like securitisation and asset reconstruction via RBI-regulated Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARCs).
evidenceBullets: Contradiction exists between SARFAESI Act Section 13(8) (borrower loses redemption right upon auction notice publication) and Rules 8 & 9 (redemption allowed until auction date).
A loan becomes an NPA when principal or interest remains overdue for more than 90 days.
The Act mandates a 60-day demand notice to borrowers before initiating asset recovery actions.
Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARCs) are registered and regulated by the RBI to buy NPAs and clean up bank balance sheets.
